topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up, bright and unwelcome, on my phone screen. It was a direct message from Mr. Harrison. My stomach immediately tightened, a cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. I hadn't expected it. I scrolled, heart thumping a frantic rhythm against my sternum, and saw a recent post he’d made—a picture of a weekend hike. A wave of awkwardness washed over me, making my hands feel clammy. He was always so composed in class, a pillar of quiet authority, and now this—this digital reach—felt like a sudden, unwelcome invasion of my personal space. I quickly minimized the app, shoving my phone deep into my backpack, trying to force a casual shrug onto my shoulders, but my breath hitched in my throat.

[story 2]

I found myself staring at my laptop screen, the glow reflecting in my wide, slightly unfocused eyes. It was a profile picture of Mr. Davies, taken at a school event. A ripple of unease spread through me, a strange churning in my gut that made my palms slick against the cool metal of the keyboard. Every time I looked away, I felt a persistent need to check the notifications again, a compulsion I couldn't seem to shake. The silence of my room amplified the sound of my own shallow breathing. I traced the outline of his name on the screen, a tremor running up my arm. It wasn't just that he was online; it was the sudden, sharp awareness that this connection, this digital thread, was somehow misplaced, exposing something I wasn't prepared to handle.

[story 3]

When I saw the tag on the shared photo, a flush crept up my neck, warm and undeniable. It was Mr. Chen. My mind raced, cycling through scenarios—what did this mean? Did he see something in my work? The air in the hallway suddenly felt thick, heavy, as if the light had dimmed. I gripped the strap of my messenger bag so tightly my knuckles turned white. I managed a weak, forced smile when a classmate approached, but inside, a frantic energy was buzzing, a desperate urge to disappear. I quickly pulled out my phone, not to engage, but just to stare at the screen, the image of his profile dominating my focus, a heavy weight settling squarely on my chest.
